children.name: Bufo
children.description: The archetypal toads of Eurasia — warty, terrestrial, slow-moving, and largely nocturnal. The Common Toad is Sweden's only toad, gathering in vast numbers at traditional breeding ponds each spring, often returning to the exact pond where it was born. Toxic parotoid glands behind the eyes deter most predators. Unlike frogs, toads walk rather than hop.

children.name: Bufo bufo
children.description: The quintessential Swedish toad; migrates hundreds of metres to natal pond in early spring; toxic parotoid glands produce bufadienolide poisons that deter most predators; male smaller than female and clasps her for days during amplexus.

children.name: Bufo spinosus
children.description: Recently separated from B. bufo; distinguished by more prominent dorsal tubercles; SW Europe.

children.name: Bufo gargarizans
children.description: Commonest toad across East Asia; skin secretions used in traditional Chinese medicine.

children.name: Bufo verrucosissimus
children.description: A large warty toad of the Caucasus and northeastern Anatolia, inhabiting humid montane forests near streams.

children.name: Bufo japonicus
children.description: Widespread across Japan; a large terrestrial toad that breeds explosively in late-winter ponds.

children.name: Bufo bankorensis
children.description: Endemic to Taiwan from lowlands to high mountains; reddish warts on the dorsum.

children.name: Bufo torrenticola
children.description: A Japanese toad adapted to fast mountain streams, where its tadpoles develop sucker-like mouths to cling to rocks.

children.name: Anaxyrus
children.description: The New World radiation of true toads.

children.name: Anaxyrus americanus
children.description: Familiar garden visitor across eastern North America; musical trill lasts up to 30 seconds.

children.name: Anaxyrus boreas
children.description: Declining in western mountain lakes due to chytrid fungus; once aggregated in vast numbers.

children.name: Anaxyrus fowleri
children.description: Eastern North American toad of sandy habitats; short, nasal 'waaah' call; named after naturalist Samuel Page Fowler.

children.name: Anaxyrus woodhousii
children.description: Wide-ranging western North American toad with a pale mid-dorsal stripe; named for naturalist Samuel Washington Woodhouse.

children.name: Anaxyrus terrestris
children.description: Toad of the southeastern US with prominent cranial crests ending in knobs; common across the sandy coastal plain.

children.name: Anaxyrus houstonensis
children.description: Critically endangered toad endemic to a few counties in Texas; among the first US amphibians listed under the Endangered Species Act.

children.name: Anaxyrus quercicus
children.description: Smallest North American toad at under 33 mm; pine-oak woodlands of the southeastern US; call resembles a chick's peep.

children.name: Rhinella
children.description: Large tropical toads.

children.name: Rhinella marina
children.description: Catastrophically invasive in Australia, Hawaii, and the Caribbean; toxic skin kills native predators; females lay up to 35,000 eggs per clutch; a cautionary tale in biocontrol.

children.name: Rhinella icterica
children.description: Large toad of southeastern Brazil, Argentina and Paraguay with yellowish flanks; common in Atlantic Forest.

children.name: Rhinella schneideri
children.description: One of South America's largest toads, with enormous parotoid glands; inhabits the Cerrado and Chaco savannas.

children.name: Epidalea
children.description: Small toads with distinctive markings.

children.name: Epidalea calamita
children.description: A narrow yellow dorsal stripe and the loudest call of any European amphibian, audible for kilometres; runs rather than hops; rare in southernmost Sweden; rapidly drying breeding pools allow it to outcompete other species.

children.name: Atelopus
children.description: The harlequin toads — small, brightly coloured, diurnal stream toads of the Neotropics, among the amphibians worst hit by the chytrid fungus.

children.name: Atelopus zeteki
children.description: Critically endangered (likely extinct in the wild) golden toad endemic to Panama; a national symbol; secretes potent zetekitoxin.

children.name: Atelopus varius
children.description: Critically endangered harlequin toad of Costa Rica and Panama; once thought extinct, rediscovered in tiny relict populations.

children.name: Peltophryne
children.description: Caribbean toads radiating across the Greater Antilles, several of which are threatened island endemics.

children.name: Peltophryne lemur
children.description: Critically endangered toad endemic to Puerto Rico; the focus of a major captive-breeding and reintroduction programme.

children.name: Nimbaphrynoides
children.description: A remarkable West African toad genus exhibiting true viviparity — among the only frogs to give live birth to fully formed young.

children.name: Nimbaphrynoides occidentalis
children.description: Critically endangered toad restricted to the high-altitude grasslands of Mount Nimba; one of very few frogs that gives birth to live young.

children.name: Duttaphrynus
children.description: Asian toads separated from Bufo, including the ubiquitous common toad of South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Duttaphrynus melanostictus
children.description: The most widespread toad of South and Southeast Asia; commensal with humans; recently invasive in Madagascar, where it threatens native predators.

children.name: Duttaphrynus himalayanus
children.description: A robust toad of the Himalayan foothills, found in cool, moist montane habitats up to high elevations.

children.name: Incilius
children.description: Central American toads, including the legendary golden toad whose extinction became an emblem of the global amphibian crisis.

children.name: Incilius periglenes
children.description: Brilliant orange cloud-forest toad of Monteverde, Costa Rica, last seen in 1989 and now extinct; an iconic early casualty of the amphibian decline crisis.
